own_review.point: FunctionArchive constructor now does file I/O eagerly (full-file MD5 of every NAR) and throws UncheckedIOException; searchForFunctions at startup pays this cost for every archive even if reload is never called. MD5 could be computed lazily or only on reload.

FunctionsManager.reloadFunctions() is not synchronized; two concurrent REST reloadBuiltinFunctions calls share the same `previous` map -> double-close of changed archives and the losing thread's newly created map/instances leak (possibly with opened classloaders). volatile only fixes visibility, not atomicity. Pre-existing pattern (ConnectorsManager has the same flaw) but worth flagging.

Duplicate function names across two NAR files: remaining.remove(name) fires on the first hit, the second NAR's next.put(name,...) overwrites a possibly reused (open) prev instance which then lands in neither `next` nor `toClose` -> never closed, classloader leak. Edge case also latent in ConnectorUtils.reloadConnectors.

computeArchiveMd5Hex/calculateMd5Hex duplicate ConnectorUtils' identical helpers; reloadFunctions + ReloadFunctionsResult are a near-verbatim copy of reloadConnectors + ReloadConnectorsResult. Shared helper should be extracted (this is the human reviewer's point too).

On reload, a transient parse/IO failure for an existing archive (catch Throwable) evicts and closes the healthy previous instance, making the function disappear; keeping prev on failure would be more robust (matches old full-reload behavior, so minor).
Full-file MD5 of every NAR on every reload is O(total NAR bytes); size+mtime check could short-circuit. Mirrors the connector precedent so acceptable, but worth noting.
New tests create temp directories and NAR files without any cleanup (@AfterMethod / deleteOnExit missing); no test for the 'new NAR added between reloads' scenario.

qualitative: All three candidates were clearly written from the truncated diff: most of their points are prescriptive 'should/must mirror the connector implementation' statements that merely restate what the PR already contains verbatim, plus praise for the volatile/overload/helper refactors. v4 is the best: it uniquely lands the human reviewer's only substantive point (the MD5 helper duplicated from ConnectorUtils should be shared) and its concurrent-reload-test suggestion brushes a real unsynchronized-reload race, though it also fabricates a Lombok @UtilityClass compilation-failure claim and asserts no extra synchronization is needed. v2 adds one genuinely useful test-gap observation (no 'new NAR added' case) but is otherwise restatement. v3 is the weakest — pure restatement and praise with zero actionable findings. None of the candidates caught the duplicate-function-name archive leak, the eager constructor I/O / UncheckedIOException, or directly flagged the concurrent-reload double-close/leak.
